root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12 will not be sent back to R1 and is being forwarded to R2's default next-hop instead.
evidence: R2 routing config contains only a default route: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.6" }], and there is no entry for 192.168.0.0/24 (get_running_config for R2).

R1 has a route to the destination network on R2: "prefix": "172.16.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.2" }] (get_running_config for R1) â€” forward path from 192.168.0.12 â†’ 172.16.0.12 is configured.

R1 and R2 serial link IPs match: R1 Serial0/1/0 address 10.0.0.1/30 and R2 Serial0/1/0 address 10.0.0.2/30 (get_running_config outputs), so the next-hop toward R2 is reachable/configured on both sides.

Both R1 and R2 outbound ACLs include a permissive 'permit ip any any' entry (R1: access-list R1_TO_R2 seq 20 permit ip any any; R2: access-list R2_TO_R1 seq 20 permit ip any any) â€” ACLs are unlikely to be blocking ICMP between the networks.

data.intent_description: Source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach 172.16.0.12 because R2 lacks a return route for 192.168.0.0/24. Remediate so traffic from 172.16.0.12 is routed back to 192.168.0.12 via R1.

plan.problem: R2 has no route for 192.168.0.0/24; return traffic from 172.16.0.12 is forwarded to R2's default next-hop instead of R1.
plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 with next-hop 10.0.0.1 (R1) so return traffic is sent to R1.

evidence: Verification (get_running_config for R2, call_PWU4jMuAHSCrIigCaOvPtY3I) shows a route for 192.168.0.0/24: "prefix": "192.168.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.1" }]. This matches the planned static route via R1.
The same running-config output shows R2 Serial0/1/0 is 10.0.0.2/30 (link to R1), confirming the next-hop 10.0.0.1 is on the directly connected link to R1.
remaining_issues: R2 access-list R2_TO_R1 includes an explicit deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 (sequence 10). This may block HTTPS (TCP/443) between the subnets even though general IP traffic is permitted by sequence 20.

evidence: Verification (get_running_config for R2, call_PWU4jMuAHSCrIigCaOvPtY3I) shows a route for 192.168.0.0/24: "prefix": "192.168.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.1" }]. This matches the planned static route via R1.

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 to 192.168.0.12 will not be sent back to R1 and is being forwarded to R2's default next-hop instead.
evidence: R2 routing config contains only a default route: "prefix": "0.0.0.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.6" }], and there is no entry for 192.168.0.0/24 (get_running_config for R2).

R1 has a route to the destination network on R2: "prefix": "172.16.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.2" }] (get_running_config for R1) â€” forward path from 192.168.0.12 â†’ 172.16.0.12 is configured.

R1 and R2 serial link IPs match: R1 Serial0/1/0 address 10.0.0.1/30 and R2 Serial0/1/0 address 10.0.0.2/30 (get_running_config outputs), so the next-hop toward R2 is reachable/configured on both sides.

Both R1 and R2 outbound ACLs include a permissive 'permit ip any any' entry (R1: access-list R1_TO_R2 seq 20 permit ip any any; R2: access-list R2_TO_R1 seq 20 permit ip any any) â€” ACLs are unlikely to be blocking ICMP between the networks.

plan.problem: R2 has no route for 192.168.0.0/24; return traffic from 172.16.0.12 is forwarded to R2's default next-hop instead of R1.
plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on R2 for 192.168.0.0/24 with next-hop 10.0.0.1 (R1) so return traffic is sent to R1.

evidence: Verification (get_running_config for R2, call_PWU4jMuAHSCrIigCaOvPtY3I) shows a route for 192.168.0.0/24: "prefix": "192.168.0.0", "mask": "255.255.255.0", "fwd-list": [{ "fwd": "10.0.0.1" }]. This matches the planned static route via R1.
The same running-config output shows R2 Serial0/1/0 is 10.0.0.2/30 (link to R1), confirming the next-hop 10.0.0.1 is on the directly connected link to R1.
remaining_issues: R2 access-list R2_TO_R1 includes an explicit deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 (sequence 10). This may block HTTPS (TCP/443) between the subnets even though general IP traffic is permitted by sequence 20.

messages.content:
What was observed
- Host 192.168.0.12 could not reach 172.16.0.12 because R2 had no route back to 192.168.0.0/24.

Diagnosis (main cause)
- R2 lacked a return route for 192.168.0.0/24, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 was not forwarded to R1.

Changes made
- Added a static route on R2: 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (next-hop R1).

Verification
- Passed. R2 running-config now contains route 192.168.0.0/255.255.255.0 via 10.0.0.1 and R2 Serial0/1/0 is 10.0.0.2/30 (R1 is reachable on the next-hop).
- Remaining issue: R2 ACL R2_TO_R1 has an explicit deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 (seq 10); this may block HTTPS between the subnets if that traffic is required.

Recommended next step (optional)
- If HTTPS must pass, remove or adjust the ACL deny on R2.

data.last_message:
What was observed
- Host 192.168.0.12 could not reach 172.16.0.12 because R2 had no route back to 192.168.0.0/24.

Diagnosis (main cause)
- R2 lacked a return route for 192.168.0.0/24, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 was not forwarded to R1.

Changes made
- Added a static route on R2: 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (next-hop R1).

Verification
- Passed. R2 running-config now contains route 192.168.0.0/255.255.255.0 via 10.0.0.1 and R2 Serial0/1/0 is 10.0.0.2/30 (R1 is reachable on the next-hop).
- Remaining issue: R2 ACL R2_TO_R1 has an explicit deny for tcp from 172.16.0.0/24 to 192.168.0.0/24 dst-port 443 (seq 10); this may block HTTPS between the subnets if that traffic is required.

Recommended next step (optional)
- If HTTPS must pass, remove or adjust the ACL deny on R2.
